text: Peak impact, active, and braking ground reaction forces are not significantly different between runners with tibial stress fracture history and healthy controls based on meta-analytic evidence.
population: runners with tibial stress fracture vs controls

text: Discrete ground reaction force variables alone are insufficient to identify runners at risk for tibial stress fracture.
Runners with tibial stress fracture history tend to exhibit greater peak tibial anterior tensile stress and peak posterior compressive stress compared to controls.
population: runners with tibial stress fracture vs controls

text: Greater peak tibial axial acceleration during running is associated with tibial stress fracture history.
population: runners with tibial stress fracture vs controls

text: Greater peak rearfoot eversion during running is associated with tibial stress fracture history.
population: runners with tibial stress fracture vs controls

text: Greater hip adduction during running is associated with tibial stress fracture history.
population: runners with tibial stress fracture vs controls

text: Tibial stress fracture is an overuse injury with a long recovery period, making prevention through biomechanical screening a priority for high-mileage runners.
